title: The 10 Best Horror Movies So Far This Year. Plus: ‘Renfield,’ ‘The Pope’s Exorcist,’ and ‘Evil Dead Rise.’
format: Chris Ryan joins Sean to run through a busy April: Renfield, The Pope's Exorcist, and Evil Dead Rise. They argue about who's actually running horror right now, get into the Skinamarink/Outwaters strain of anti-narrative horror, and work through a batch of VOD deep cuts. Closes with Daniel Goldhaber and Ariella Barer on How to Blow Up a Pipeline.

segments.heading: Renfield, The Pope's Exorcist, and Evil Dead Rise
segments.note: four horror movies with not much in common

films.title: Renfield
films.note: Chris McKay's historic-IP reboot: Dracula (Nicolas Cage) as a narcissistic, toxic boss, told through his familiar. Sean liked the pieces (Cage "comes to play") but the ultraviolet-gangster-movie, cop-drama, and rom-com strands never hang together. Came in behind The Pope's Exorcist at the box office; neither cracked $10 million opening weekend.

films.title: The Pope's Exorcist
films.note: Julius Avery's based-on-a-true-story exorcism procedural, inspired by William Friedkin's documentary about Father Amorth. Russell Crowe, fully committed and doing his own Italian and Spanish, elevates a silly movie that doesn't seem to know it's silly: more Richard Burton late-period professionalism than horror.

films.title: Evil Dead Rise
films.note: Lee Cronin's fifth Evil Dead movie, set in an earthquake-cracked LA apartment building instead of a cabin. Possibly the goriest studio horror movie either host has seen in years ("fun torture porn"), and it played well to a packed Burbank crowd. Alyssa Sutherland's physical performance as the mother gets a specific shout-out.

films.title: Beau Is Afraid
films.note: Ari Aster's three-hour nightmare comedy, expanding wide the same week. Sean is saving the full discussion for a later episode with Adam Naiman, but flags it as barely horror at all, the same open question raised by Hereditary and Midsommar.

segments.heading: Is There a King of Horror Right Now?
segments.note: running down the directors staking a claim

films.title: Malignant
films.note: James Wan, the guy Sean and Chris agree currently holds the belt. Watched cold at 9 a.m. it didn't land; a rewatch a month later revealed a movie that's a total homage to the stuff Wan loves.

films.title: The Invisible Man
films.note: Leigh Whannell, Wan's longtime collaborator. One of the more underrated movies of the pandemic era and the last real hit before lockdown.

films.title: Doctor Sleep
films.note: Mike Flanagan, whom Chris ranks in the top 1% of horror directing over the last decade before he mostly moved to television.

films.title: Hush
films.note: Flanagan again, one of the core feature credits before the shift to Netflix limited series.

films.title: Ouija: Origin of Evil
films.note: Another Flanagan studio job on the resume.

films.title: The Babadook
films.note: Jennifer Kent's debut. Sean says it looked like she'd be next up before she went a different direction entirely.

films.title: The Nightingale
films.note: Kent's follow-up, a real gear-shift from The Babadook. Both hosts rate it highly; she hasn't made a feature since.

films.title: Synchronic
films.note: Benson and Moorhead's Anthony Mackie/Jamie Dornan bid at something closer to mainstream, though still an indie at heart.

films.title: Host
films.note: Rob Savage's Zoom-horror COVID movie, near-universally liked. Sean calls Savage a director he's "scouting."

films.title: Dashcam
films.note: Savage's follow-up, which Sean found borderline unwatchable but figures that was the point.

films.title: X
films.note: Ti West (called "Ty West" on air), back from years of mostly TV work. Sean rates it as sweet-spot filmmaking: West knows the history of the genre and still does his own spin on it.

films.title: Pearl
films.note: West's companion piece. Mia Goth's performance was pushed hard for awards attention that never materialized.

films.title: The Sacrament
films.note: Chris calls this West's most underrated movie.

segments.heading: Skinamarink, The Outwaters, and What Comes After Elevated Horror
segments.note: phantasmagoric, abstract, and hard to sit through on purpose

films.title: Skinamarink
films.note: Kyle Edward Ball's micro-budget breakout. Chris and Sean disagree: Sean is still turning it over after two viewings and thinks a third might change his mind. Best experienced alone, scared, in the dark; loses power outside that context.

films.title: The Outwaters
films.note: Robbie Banfitch's $15,000 desert nightmare: a conventional found-footage setup that dissolves into a gory, time-traveling, POV wormhole. Some of it lands, some of it "felt unwatchable," but Sean respects the vision on that budget regardless.

films.title: Infinity Pool
films.note: Brandon Cronenberg's follow-up to Possessor: more linear and psychedelic than truly experimental, in their view.

films.title: Possessor
films.note: The Cronenberg film they'd actually put closer to the Skinamarink/Outwaters camp, and it reviewed better than Infinity Pool.

segments.heading: VOD Corner
segments.note: Chris's deep cuts from a softer year for streaming horror

films.title: Sick
films.note: Directed by John Hyams, an action specialist (the Universal Soldier sequels) doing a COVID-set slasher. The fight choreography is a level above anything else in the genre right now, and the twist works. Streaming on Peacock; possibly Chris's favorite movie of the year.

films.title: Candyland
films.note: John Swab's Oklahoma-truck-stop slasher, half grindhouse, half mumblecore, with William Baldwin "on his Too Old to Die Young grind." Not good but enjoyed anyway; Sean's wife had a theory about the title.

films.title: There's Something Wrong with the Children
films.note: Roxanne Benjamin (Body at Brighton Rock, Southbound) directs Zach Gilford in a childless-couple-on-vacation-with-friends'-kids story. Sean wanted more mythology; the inciting event stays largely unexplained.

films.title: Consecration
films.note: Half-successful religious horror with a strong Jena Malone performance carrying a familiar story.

films.title: Huesera: The Bone Woman
films.note: Grouped with Consecration as part of the same wave of birth-and-loss horror; also only half-successful for Chris despite a strong lead performance.

films.title: Baby Ruby
films.note: Another entry in the current run of movies about birth, loss, and parenthood as horror.

films.title: The Night House
films.note: David Bruckner and Rebecca Hall, cited as an example of trauma moving from subtext to text in recent horror.

films.title: Resurrection
films.note: Rebecca Hall again, in the same trauma-forward mold as The Night House.

segments.heading: What's Coming Next
segments.note: the IP moment, and a few genuine wild cards

films.title: The Exorcist: Believer
films.note: David Gordon Green moving straight from his Halloween trilogy into an Exorcist legacy sequel with Leslie Odom Jr.

films.title: The Last Voyage of the Demeter
films.note: The Dracula chapter nobody adapts, everything that happens aboard the ship, with Corey Hawkins and Liam Cunningham. Sean liked the trailer: "low-grade Master and Commander with Dracula on the ship."

films.title: Talk to Me
films.note: Sean already flagged this one from Sundance; now that A24 has it, he's calling it the horror phenomenon of the summer, out at the end of July.

films.title: MaXXXine
films.note: Ti West's third film in the X trilogy, set in '80s LA porn, with a cast that includes Elizabeth Debicki, Giancarlo Esposito, Michelle Monaghan, Lily Collins, Bobby Cannavale, Halsey, and Kevin Bacon.

films.title: The Boogeyman
films.note: Rob Savage's Stephen King adaptation, with Chris Messina. The movie Sean says he's most looking forward to next.

films.title: Apartment 7A
films.note: A mysterious, undated Paramount production revealed to be a Rosemary's Baby prequel/sequel: Julia Garner and Diane Wiest, directed by Natalie Erika James (Relic), produced by John Krasinski.

films.title: The Nun II
films.note: Neither host loved the first one, but they're curious.

films.title: Saw X
films.note: Sean was never a Saw guy after the first one's Achilles-tendon scene; Chris passes too.

films.title: The Strangers: Chapter 1
films.note: Renny Harlin reboots The Strangers. Both hosts are surprised he's still directing.

films.title: Nightbitch
films.note: Marielle Heller's adaptation of Rachel Yoder's novel about a woman who turns into a dog at night, with Amy Adams and Scoot McNairy. Billed as comedy, but could tip horror.

films.title: V/H/S/85
films.note: Sean will keep watching this anthology series for as long as they keep making them.

films.title: Salem's Lot
films.note: Screenwriter Gary Dauberman (Annabelle, It) directing his own Stephen King adaptation.

films.title: Do Not Watch
films.note: A mystery project from an unnamed director, plot kept under wraps aside from a "decade-spanning" mystery called the Bunker Anomaly. Sean's most intrigued-but-nervous pick of the preview.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.guest: Daniel Goldhaber and Ariella Barer on

films.title: How to Blow Up a Pipeline
films.note: Adapted from Andreas Malm's nonfiction book by Goldhaber, Barer, and Jordan Scholl into an Ocean's Eleven-style heist thriller. Self-funded early on with money drained from their own accounts and a Cannes party crash that got them banned from the next one's security. Built as a deliberately aspirational story about leftist direct action, a rebuttal to films like First Reformed and Night Moves, which Goldhaber argues concede that resistance is futile.

films.title: Cam
films.note: Goldhaber's directorial debut, referenced as the project that first connected him with Barer before their own follow-up fell apart at the start of COVID lockdown.

films.title: Faces of Death
films.note: Goldhaber's next film for Legendary, a reimagining of the found-footage shockumentary, starring Barbie Ferreira, Josie Totah, and Bakr Montgomery. They start shooting in three days.

films.title: Reservoir Dogs
films.note: Barer's biggest structural and tonal touchstone for Pipeline.

films.title: The Battle of Algiers
films.note: Named alongside Reservoir Dogs as a reference point.

films.title: Thief
films.note: Michael Mann cited as an influence on the film's craft.

films.title: Nocturama
films.note: Barer's pick for capturing the disillusionment of youth and rebellion.

films.title: Woman at War
films.note: Barer's personal favorite among the reference points, an Icelandic film about environmental sabotage.

films.title: Four Lions
films.note: A late but major discovery for both: Chris Morris's black comedy about a group's camaraderie and ideology, filtered through humor. Writer Sam Bain gave the Pipeline team notes on writing characters far outside their own experience.

films.title: Empty Metal
films.note: Cited as an example of the more overtly "revolutionary form" movie Goldhaber and Barer respect but didn't want to make themselves.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: Goldhaber's counterexample: a film he thinks walks away suggesting there's nothing to be done about climate change, which he sees as its own kind of moral compromise.

films.title: Night Moves
films.note: Grouped with First Reformed as a film about eco-sabotage that lands on futility rather than possibility.

films.title: The Conformist
films.note: Goldhaber's answer to "last great thing you saw": a first-time watch he's now obsessed with, and another model for political filmmaking he wants to pursue.

films.title: Strange Days
films.note: Kathryn Bigelow, named in the same breath as The Conformist: one of Goldhaber's favorite movies.

TV: Guillermo del Toro's Cabinet of Curiosities
